title: The Dead Man's Route
prompt: Extreme wide shot of a dimly lit Fleet Command briefing room, massive holographic star map casting blue and amber light across rows of empty chairs. A single figure, Kael Vasik, sits in the center foreground — a lean man in his late twenties with sharp jaw-length black hair swept back, a thin scar tracing his left cheekbone, wearing a fitted charcoal flight suit with silver piping and a faded unit patch on his right shoulder. Natural human facial features, well-defined human nose, clear skin catching the holographic glow. The star map shows a red corridor through enemy territory with two blinking skull icons marking the previous pilots who flew it. Cool blue and deep amber lighting, cinematic depth with the vast room stretching into shadow behind him. Atmosphere of isolation and dread.
narrative: Kael Vasik receives his briefing alone. Two courier pilots before him — Haren and Soltz — are both dead. Same route, same mission. Deliver the encrypted data packet through enemy-held space to the forward fleet. Fleet Command calls it "critical." Nobody calls it survivable.
narration: They gave me the briefing in a room built for fifty. I sat alone. Two names already scratched off the roster — Haren, Soltz. Both flew the same corridor. Both are gone. The packet sits on the table in front of me. Encrypted. Critical. That is the word they kept using. Critical. Not "survivable." Not "routine." Critical. I picked it up anyway. Because that is what couriers do when everyone else is already dead. I pressed my thumb to the manifest and the light turned green. Authorization confirmed. Pilot Kael Vasik. Third attempt. I almost laughed. Third attempt. Like I was a software patch instead of a man with a name.

title: Into the Dark
prompt: Wide shot from behind and slightly above, showing Kael Vasik climbing into the cockpit of a sleek, angular single-pilot courier ship — the designation "KD-9 Vesper" stenciled in white on the dark gunmetal hull. His flight suit catches the hangar floodlights, which cast long sharp shadows across the concrete floor. Kael's black hair is pushed back, his profile visible as he grips the cockpit rail with one gloved hand, the other holding the data packet against his chest. The hangar is vast and mostly empty, with maintenance drones crawling along the far walls and a single red departure beacon blinking above the open launch bay doors, beyond which stars glitter against infinite black. Color palette of cold steel grays, warm amber hangar lights, and the cold white-blue of starlight. Cinematic, lonely, determined.
narrative: The hangar feels too quiet. No send-off crew. No telemetry officer wishing him luck. Just Kael, the Vesper, and the cold hum of the launch bay cycling open. He seals the cockpit. Straps in. Inserts the data packet into the ship's secure vault. The console comes alive with route markers — the same red corridor Haren and Soltz never returned from.
narration: The Vesper is a coffin with wings. I say that with love — she is fast, quiet, and built to not be seen. Which is the only reason anyone survives the Run. I climb in. Seal the canopy. The data packet clicks into the vault behind my seat and the console paints my route in red. Red. Not green. Not yellow. Red. Fleet Command knows what that means. I know what that means. The bay doors peel open and the stars are waiting. I push the throttle forward and the hangar falls away behind me like a memory I am choosing to forget.

title: First Warning
prompt: Medium shot inside the cockpit, Kael Vasik visible from the waist up, his gloved hands gripping the flight stick as his eyes scan a holographic radar display projected in front of him. His face shows controlled tension — jaw tight, brow furrowed, the scar on his left cheekbone catching the green instrument light. A well-defined human nose and natural human facial features are visible in the instrument glow. The cockpit is tight and utilitarian, layered with blinking switches and status readouts. A warning indicator on the radar pulses red — something is tracking him, but the signature doesn't match any known enemy patrol pattern. Cool green and red instrument lighting reflects off the visor of his flight helmet resting on the console beside him. Tight, claustrophobic framing with depth created by the glowing holographic displays.
narrative: Twelve hours into the Run. Kael notices something wrong on the radar. A shadow contact — too deliberate to be debris, too quiet to be a standard patrol. Someone knows his exact corridor. He checks the nav data again. The coordinates were classified. Only Fleet Command had them. The shadow follows.
narration: Twelve hours. No contact. That alone should have told me something was wrong. The Run passes through three enemy sectors — you do not go twelve hours without picking up at least a patrol signature. Then I see it. A shadow on the edge of my radar. Not a patrol. Not debris. A tail. Precise. Patient. Someone fed them my corridor. My exact heading. I pull up the nav data and stare at the encrypted coordinates. Classified. Eyes-only distribution. Which means whoever sold me out sits behind a Fleet Command desk. My hands tighten on the stick. The shadow does not break off. It just follows.

title: Nebula Dive
prompt: Low angle shot looking up at Kael's courier ship, the Vesper, as it banks hard into a massive swirling nebula — clouds of violet, crimson, and deep indigo churning like a living storm. The ship's angular silhouette cuts against the nebula's glow, engine trails burning orange as Kael pushes the throttle to maximum. In the foreground, fragmented rock and ice debris tumbles past the camera, blurred with motion. The nebula dominates the upper two-thirds of the frame, vast and beautiful and lethal, with occasional crackling discharges of electrical energy illuminating the interior clouds. The Vesper is small against the enormity — a needle diving into a hurricane. Palette of deep purples, hot crimsons, electric blues, and engine-orange. Epic scale, desperate velocity, the sense of flying into a storm with no guarantee of coming out.
narrative: Kael has one move — the Kreth Nebula. Lethal electromagnetic interference that will shred unshielded sensors. His shadow will lose him in there. Or the nebula will kill him first. He rolls the Vesper into a hard bank and dives straight in. Alarms scream. The hull groans. The stars vanish behind curtains of churning violet gas.
narration: The nebula is suicide. Everyone knows that. The electromagnetic interference will tear your sensors apart, scramble your nav, turn your cockpit into a light show while your hull cracks like eggshell. That is exactly why I am going in. Because whoever is tailing me has orders to keep me alive long enough to confirm my cargo — and they cannot follow me through that. I bank hard. The Vesper screams in protest. Every alarm in the cockpit fires at once. The nebula swallows us whole and the stars disappear behind walls of violet fire. Inside the cloud, I am blind. But so are they.

title: Relay Station Ghost
prompt: Over-the-shoulder shot from behind Kael Vasik as he stands in the airlock of a battered deep-space relay station, looking into a dimly lit interior corridor. His flight suit is scuffed and dirty from the nebula transit, his black hair disheveled, one glove missing. The station's corridor stretches ahead — emergency lighting flickers red, panels hang loose from the ceiling, and there are scorch marks on the bulkhead walls. A friendly contact signal blinks on a cracked console visible ahead — the station is supposed to be allied, a resupply point on the Run. But something is wrong: no welcoming crew, no active life support hum, just silence. Kael's hand rests on a sidearm holstered at his hip. The framing uses his shoulder and head as a silhouette frame, the dark corridor stretching into uncertain red-lit distance. Color palette of muted grays, emergency reds, and the cold blue of Kael's suit lights. Tense, wrong-feeling atmosphere.
narrative: Station Acreon-7 — the midway resupply point. It should be crawling with Fleet logistics crew. Instead, it is dark. Silent. Kael docks the Vesper and steps into the airlock. The station's comm system chirps a friendly signal, but the corridors are empty. Scorch marks on the walls. Someone was here. Recently.
narration: Acreon-7. The midpoint. Fuel, rest, a hot meal — that is what this station is supposed to be. I dock the Vesper and the airlock opens onto nothing. Just red emergency lighting and silence. The comm system chirps at me like nothing is wrong. Friendly signal. Everything fine. But the bulkheads are scorched. Panels torn down. I draw my sidearm. The barrel shakes — I will not pretend it does not. I move deeper into the corridor and the silence gets louder with every step. Whatever happened here happened to whoever the traitor wanted to disappear next. And I am walking right into the same room.

title: The Setup
prompt: Dutch angle, tilted and unsettling, showing the interior of the station's operations room. Kael Vasik stands mid-frame, his sidearm raised, facing a Fleet officer in a clean uniform — Commander Eldra Noss, a tall woman with silver-streaked auburn hair pulled tight, sharp cheekbones, a well-defined human nose, and cold natural human facial features. She stands behind an overturned console with her hands slightly raised, but her posture is too calm, too composed for someone supposedly stranded. Two armed figures in unmarked tactical armor are partially visible in the shadows behind her, weapons not yet raised. The room is trashed — overturned chairs, sparking conduits, a cracked viewport showing the nebula outside. The dutch angle creates a sense of wrongness and betrayal. Harsh red and blue lighting from emergency systems and the viewport nebula glow. Tension hangs in the air like static.
narrative: Commander Eldra Noss. Fleet Intelligence. She claims her team was ambushed, stranded here. She asks to see the data packet — for "verification purposes." Kael hesitates. Something about her story does not add up. The ambush happened before his route was even assigned. Before two pilots died. Then he sees the shadows behind her. Unmarked armor. Not Fleet.
narration: Commander Noss. I know her name. Fleet Intelligence — the people who planned this entire Run. She says her team was hit. Stranded. Needs my help. She smiles when she asks to see the packet. That is when I know. The timing is wrong. Her team was ambushed before my route was assigned. Before Haren and Soltz died. She knew the corridor because she built it. Built it to be a killing field. I see the shadows move behind her. Unmarked armor. Not Fleet. Not allied. My finger moves to the trigger. Her smile gets wider. "Pilot Vasik," she says. "You are smarter than the last two."

title: Breakout
prompt: Medium close-up, chest-up framing of Kael Vasik as he dives sideways through a collapsing blast door, his sidearm firing behind him in a streak of bright muzzle flash. His face is caught mid-strain — teeth gritted, eyes wide, the scar on his cheekbone stark against the sweat on his skin. A well-defined human nose and natural human facial features are visible in the harsh light. Behind him through the narrowing gap of the blast door, Commander Noss's operatives are visible — one hit, one raising a rifle. Sparks and debris rain down as the door grinds shut. The corridor ahead is dark except for the pulsing blue guide lights along the floor leading back to the docking bay. Kael's flight suit is torn at the shoulder, his sidearm smoking. Color palette of explosive orange muzzle flash, cold blue corridor lights, and deep shadow. Fast, violent, desperate motion.
narrative: Kael fires. Drops the first operative. The second returns fire — a round clips the bulkhead centimeters from his head. He sprints. The blast door is closing. He slides through the gap as it seals, the Commander's voice cutting through the intercom behind him: "He has the packet. Seal the dock." Kael runs.
narration: I fire twice. The first one drops. The second raises his rifle and I am already moving — because standing still is how Haren died, standing still is how Soltz died, and I refuse to be the third name on a briefing room wall. The blast door is closing. I throw myself through the gap and the metal grinds shut behind me like a jaw snapping. Noss's voice follows me through the comm. She is not even angry. She is calm. That scares me more than the bullets. I run for the docking bay. The Vesper is twenty meters away. My lungs burn. The corridor lights pulse blue like a heartbeat counting down.

title: Canyon of Glass
prompt: Extreme wide shot of a spectacular and terrifying asteroid canyon — jagged crystalline formations jutting from both sides like the teeth of a colossal mouth, their surfaces catching and refracting the distant starlight into razor-sharp prismatic reflections. Kael's Vesper streaks through the narrow center passage at reckless speed, engine trails leaving glowing orange contrails that dissipate against the crystal walls. Behind him, three enemy interceptors in pursuit — sleek, angular, predatory, their weapons firing bright lances of green energy that shatter crystal formations on impact, sending cascades of glittering debris into the canyon. The scale is immense — the Vesper is a tiny dart threading between walls that stretch hundreds of meters high. Camera positioned high and wide, showing the full terrifying scope of the chase. Palette of crystal whites and blues, weapon-green energy blasts, engine-orange contrails, and deep space black beyond the canyon mouth. Breakneck velocity, impossible odds, cinematic grandeur.
narrative: The crystal canyons of Vehl. Kael's last shortcut before open space and the forward fleet. The interceptors found him fast — Noss must have scrambled them the moment he broke dock. The canyon is too narrow for them to fire freely, but Kael is outrunning physics. Crystal spires shatter around him as enemy fire misses by meters. The Vesper's hull groans. The canyon tightens.
narration: Three of them. Fast. Armed. And behind them, the relay station is already broadcasting my position to every enemy asset in the sector. I dive into the canyons because it is the only play — the crystal formations are too tight for their formation flying, too dense for their targeting locks to track clean. It is also too tight for me. A green lance shatters a crystal spire ten meters off my port wing and the debris rakes across my hull like fingernails. The Vesper shakes. Alarms I cannot afford to listen to. I thread through a gap that my nav computer calls "statistically inadvisable" and I think — good. Let them try to follow me through statistically inadvisable.

title: The Truth Burns
prompt: Close-up of Kael Vasik's face inside the cockpit, lit by the amber glow of a decrypted message streaming across his HUD display. His expression is a mixture of fury and sick recognition — eyes hard, jaw clenched, the scar on his cheekbone standing out against the tension in his face. A well-defined human nose and natural human facial features are sharply defined in the warm amber light. His gloved hands are visible at the bottom of the frame, gripping the flight stick with white-knuckle force. The HUD text, partially visible as reflected light in his eyes, shows communication logs — proof that Commander Noss authorized the corridor, fed the coordinates, and signed off on the "acceptable losses" of Haren and Soltz. The cockpit canopy behind him shows stars and the faint glow of the nebula receding. Palette of furious amber and gold HUD light, cold blue starlight, and shadow. Emotional, devastating, intimate.
narrative: With the interceptors temporarily shaken, Kael decrypts a secondary data file buried inside the packet. Not just fleet intelligence — communication logs. Commander Noss authorized the corridor herself. She fed the coordinates to enemy command. Haren and Soltz were not casualties. They were sacrifices. Collateral. "Acceptable losses" in a deal Kael does not yet fully understand. The packet was never just cargo. It was bait. And he was the third fly sent into the web.
narration: The secondary file decrypts while I am running. While I am still alive. I should not read it — I should fly. But the first line catches my eye and I cannot look away. It is Noss. Her signature. Her authorization codes. She built the corridor. Fed the coordinates to enemy command through a back channel. Haren and Soltz were not pilots who failed a mission. They were pilots who were sold. "Acceptable losses." That is what she called them. Two names. Two people with families and call signs and favorite songs they played in their cockpits. Acceptable. My vision blurs. Not from fear. From rage. The packet was bait. I was the third fly. Except this fly just read the spider's mail. And the forward fleet is forty minutes away.

title: Through the Fire
prompt: Wide shot, low angle from the deck of a massive forward fleet carrier as Kael's Vesper screams in for a landing, heat shields glowing cherry-red, hull scarred and smoking from the
